title: The Billable Hour Gap Most Allied Health Practices Have Never Measured
linkedin:
Most allied health practice owners know their weekly appointment count. Far fewer have calculated the ratio of paid clinician hours to billed session hours. That gap is almost always the most direct explanation for why a busy practice finishes the year with less profit than the volume suggests.

The metric is called the billable ratio. It's calculated simply: total billed hours divided by total paid clinical hours. A physiotherapist contracted for 37.5 hours who bills 28 patient sessions sits at 75 percent. One billing 22 hours sits at 59 percent. Both might have full appointment books. The difference in what flows through to profit across a full year is not small.

What's consuming the non-billable hours? Case notes are the largest factor most owners underestimate. A psychologist seeing five clients per day generates two to two-and-a-half hours of documentation time alongside their billing. That's time on the clock with no item number attached. Supervision obligations for early-career clinicians under board registration requirements add more. Late cancellations and non-attendances, even at practices with a formal policy, leave session slots empty while the clinician cost keeps running.

The start of FY27 is the right time to build this picture. A full year of session data, clinician rosters, and payroll records are available now. The billable ratio calculated from that data tells you which clinicians are generating the most revenue per paid hour, what's absorbing the rest, and where the structural adjustments would produce the biggest improvement, without adding staff or extending hours.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

facebook:
A full appointment book in July and a profit result that feels lower than it should. It's a pattern we hear regularly from allied health practice owners.

The gap usually comes down to a number most practices have never tracked: the billable ratio. If you know how many sessions your clinicians see each week but not what percentage of their paid hours those sessions represent, you're missing the metric that actually explains the margin.

July is the natural time to look at this. FY26 data is complete, and the picture is clearer now than it will be for another twelve months.

What does your practice's utilisation look like across the team?

#AustralianBusiness #AlliedHealth
google:
New financial year, familiar question: why does a full clinical schedule not always produce the profit you expect?

For allied health practices across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria, the answer often comes down to the billable ratio: the percentage of paid clinician hours that actually convert to billed patient sessions. It's a number most practice owners have never formally calculated.

The gap between billed hours and total paid hours typically traces to case note time (often underestimated at 20 to 30 minutes per session for psychologists and complex cases), late cancellation slots that run without revenue, supervision obligations for graduate clinicians, and scheduling gaps that accumulate across the week.

With FY26 now complete, the data to build this picture properly is available for the first time. A full year of session records, rostering, and payroll gives a precise view of which clinicians are converting capacity efficiently and where structural adjustments would have the most impact.

ProfitPulse works with allied health practice owners across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria on exactly this kind of analysis, from billable ratio review through to a broader financial health check for the year ahead.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
